data.title: Seagate, Western Digital Stocks Keep Rising. Only One Is Morgan Stanley's 'Top Pick.' — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Nate Wolf

Even after their massive triple-digit jumps last year, Western Digital and Seagate Technology remain two of the most under-appreciated stocks of the artificial-intelligence boom, according to Morgan Stanley.

The investment bank reiterated Overweight ratings for the two makers of hard-disk drives, or HDDs, in a research note Monday. It lifted its price targets for Seagate to $582 from $468 and for Western Digital to $380 from $369. The one big change: Seagate replaced its rival as Morgan Stanley's "top pick."

Seagate shares jumped 4.6% to $449 in premarket trading Monday, while Western Digital rose 3.2% to $304.38. The stocks are up 56% and 71% this year, respectively.

Investors' primary concern about the HDD market is that the current surge in demand will subside at some point soon, allowing supply to catch up and price growth to moderate. Morgan Stanley isn't worried about that potential downturn given the state of data-center spending.

"This demand strength comes from a combination of persistent workload shift to the cloud and the growing consumer and enterprise adoption of AI," analyst Erik W. Woodring wrote.

While flash memory is taking a couple of points of market share from HDDs each year, HDDs continue to store around 80% of the world's cloud data. Perhaps more importantly, the rise of agentic and other advanced AI workloads means greater data generation.

Morgan Stanley doesn't see supply and demand reaching parity until 2029, a year later than it previously estimated. In a concentrated market with just a few large players, Western Digital and Seagate will stand to reap the rewards.

Why pick Seagate, then? A couple of Western Digital catalysts — selling its remaining Sandisk stake, closing the valuation gap with Seagate — already occurred. Morgan Stanley also expects Seagate's gross margins to expand faster than Western Digital's margins by about 50 basis points over the next year given its ramp-up of higher-capacity shipments.

The distinction is marginal, however.

"Our conviction on the HDD space remains higher than any end-market we cover, and STX/WDC remain our most-preferred Overweight's within our IT Hardware coverage," Woodring wrote.

Write to Nate Wolf at nate.wolf@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: SLNO: $2.9B all-cash acquisition adds VYKAT XR, boosting rare disease growth and portfolio strength
data.description:
Neurocrine will acquire Soleno for $2.9B in an all-cash deal, adding VYKAT XR, the first FDA-approved treatment for hyperphagia in Prader-Willi syndrome, to its portfolio. The acquisition is expected to accelerate revenue growth, strengthen rare disease leadership, and deliver long-term value.

data.title: Corporate insiders' stock-market moves don't match the headlines. Here's what they're seeing.
data.description:
By Mark Hulbert

A key indicator shows officers and directors are actually increasing their stakes - betting that the recent selloff is temporary

Corporate insiders, far from turning more bearish during March's stock-market decline, are slightly more bullish than they were in February.

That's good news, on the theory that insiders know more about their companies' prospects than anyone else. If the insiders had reacted to the market's selloff by increasing the pace of their selling it would have meant that they don't think the market will bounce back anytime soon - and could perhaps even fall much further.

They didn't react in that way, according to data just released by InsiderSentiment.com, a source of insider data maintained by Nejat Seyhun, a finance professor at the University of Michigan and a leading expert on interpreting the behavior of company insiders, and his son Jon Seyhun. This is illustrated in the chart below, which plots the percentage of publicly traded companies for which there was net insider buying during March. This percentage jumped to 26.4% in March from 20.9% in February.

Don't be thrown off by what appears to be a still-low percentage for March. You should know that insiders sell more often than they buy, since only by selling can they cash in the equity and option grants in their compensation packages. The ratio's average level over the past decade is 23.5%, slightly lower than the March figure.

The latest insider ratio would need to be even higher before the Seyhuns would classify it as solidly bullish. In the meantime, they consider it neutral. The positive angle is how bearish it would have been for the insiders to have instead sold aggressively in March. In an email, Nejat Seyhun said the latest insider data suggest "they do not expect the Middle East war to last too long and predict that some of the price effects that occurred in March will reverse."

Particularly revealing is the behavior of insiders in the energy sector, who presumably would be buying more of their companies' shares if they believed the Iran war would continue for some time and oil prices would climb further. Yet they are not doing so: The insider buy percentage for stocks in the energy sector fell in March to 17.5% from February's 19.1%.

The bottom line: Keep your pessimism in check. The in-the-know insiders do not think the sky is falling.

Mark Hulbert is a regular contributor to MarketWatch. His Hulbert Ratings tracks investment newsletters that pay a flat fee to be audited. He can be reached at mark@hulbertratings.com

More: April is usually a strong month for stocks - but three factors now jeopardize the market rebound

Also read: The S&P 500 could hit bottom by May - and 6,000 is the stock market's correction floor

-Mark Hulbert

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Gold steady as Trump's Iran war deadline nears
data.description:
Key points:
  • U.S., Iran study ceasefire plan as Trump's deadline nears
  • Fed minutes to be released on Wednesday
  • U.S. inflation data due this week

By Ashitha Shivaprasad

Gold prices steadied on Monday, as market participants waited to see whether ceasefire negotiations could avert an escalation in the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran as a deadline to reopen the Strait of Hormuz nears.

Spot gold was little changed at $4,669.27 per ounce by 11:22 a.m. ET (1522 GMT) after falling 1% earlier in the session. U.S. gold futures rose 0.3% to $4,694.50.

On the eve of a U.S. deadline, the United States and Iran were weighing the framework of a plan to end their five‑week-old conflict, even as Tehran pushed back against pressure to swiftly reopen the Strait of Hormuz. U.S. President Donald Trump has threatened to rain "hell" on Tehran if it did not make a deal by the end of Tuesday.

"Focus is likely to remain on the war and interest rates. If the conflict drags on, oil will grind higher amid tightening supply conditions, adding to inflationary pressures," said Bart Melek, global head of commodity strategy at TD Securities.

"That leaves central banks, particularly the Federal Reserve, with less room to ease policy and could even revive discussions about higher rates if energy prices rise further, which is negative for gold."

Oil prices inched up in choppy trading on Monday, and they have risen sharply since the conflict began.

Gold is widely regarded as a hedge against geopolitical risks and inflation, but because it yields no interest, it tends to be less attractive when interest rates are high.

Other items on investors’ radar include minutes of the Fed’s March policy meeting due on Wednesday, U.S. Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) data due on Thursday, and the Consumer Price Index (CPI) on Friday.

The U.S. central bank held rates last month and a majority of traders now see no chance of the Fed cutting interest rates this year, according to CME’s FedWatch tool.

Spot silver fell 0.9% to $72.32 per ounce, platinum lost 0.9% to $1,971.04, and palladium was down 1.1% at $1,486.03.

data.title: Adverse Weather Looms Over Brazil's Corn, Soybeans — Market Talk
data.description: Hot and dry weather threatens Brazilian corn, AgRural says in a note. Despite some rainfall last week, crops remain "under alert due to low humidity," the firm says. In parts of Paraná state "producers are already estimating their losses," AgRural says. Things are also getting worrisome in some other parts of Brazil's corn belt. To ensure good productivity, "corn still needs regular precipitation through May," AgRural says. Meanwhile, the firm says Brazil's soybean harvest has reached 82% of the planted area, down from 87% a year ago. The problem is excess humidity in some areas. On the CBOT, both corn and soybeans rise around 0.3%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: Equities rise slightly while oil dips as US and Iran weigh next steps
data.description:
Key points:
  • US, Iran weigh peace framework as Trump threatens Iran infrastructure attacks
  • Crude oil trading choppy, WTI still above $110
  • US Treasury yields little changed while dollar dips

By Sinéad Carew and Gregor Stuart Hunter

Equities rose modestly while oil prices edged lower in a choppy session on Monday as investors awaited signs of a possible resolution to the Middle East war, as the U.S. and Iran weighed the framework of a plan to end their conflict.

Markets were closed in many countries for the Easter Monday and Tomb Sweeping Day holidays. But earlier in the day, investors had perked up after Axios reported that the U.S., Iran and a group of regional mediators are discussing the terms for a potential 45-day ceasefire that could lead to a permanent end to the war, citing four U.S., Israeli and regional sources with knowledge of the talks.

But Iran rejected a ceasefire and emphasized the necessity of a permanent end to the war in a response sent via Pakistan, the official IRNA news agency said on Monday.

U.S. President Donald Trump warned of "hell" for Iran and, in an expletive-laden Easter Sunday social media post, threatened to target Iran's power plants and bridges on Tuesday unless it reopens the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly a fifth of global energy traffic passes.

"The market is on edge over this ultimatum and waiting for what comes next. Until we have some kind of concrete agreement it's hard to be fully committed to investing," said Robert Pavlik, senior portfolio manager at Dakota Wealth in Fairfield, Connecticut. "I liken it to going in about chest deep in the water. You're not fully committed but more than halfway."

On Wall Street, at 10:52 a.m. ET (1452 GMT) the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 73.57 points, or 0.13%, to 46,564.03, the S&P 500 rose 11.72 points, or 0.18%, to 6,594.44 and the Nasdaq Composite rose 57.35 points, or 0.24%, to 21,932.08.

MSCI's gauge of stocks across the globe EURONEXT:IACWI rose 1.94 points , or 0.20 %, to 996.14 .

In energy markets, U.S. crude fell 0.56% to $ 110.92 a barrel and Brent fell to $ 108.74 per barrel, down 0.27% on the day.

U.S. stocks briefly pared some gains on Monday after Institute for Supply Management data showed that the U.S. services sector growth slowed in March, while prices paid by businesses for inputs climbed to near a 3-1/2-year high, an early sign that the prolonged war with Iran was boosting inflation pressures.

In currencies, the dollar index , which measures the greenback against a basket of currencies including the yen and the euro, fell 0.37% to 99.89, with the euro up 0.33% at $1.1553.

Against the Japanese yen , the dollar strengthened 0.09% to 159.7 . The yen had flirted with the crucial 160 per dollar level after Japanese Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama on Friday put currency traders on notice, saying the government stands ready to act against speculative moves in foreign exchange markets as volatility has risen "significantly."

U.S. Treasuries were little changed on Monday, with investors caught between optimism over reports of a ceasefire plan and unease over Trump's threat to escalate strikes on Iran.

The yield on benchmark U.S. 10-year notes fell 1.3 basis points to 4.333 %, from 4.346 % late on Friday while t he 30-year bond (US30YT=RR) yield fell 1.6 basis points to 4.8897 %.

The 2-year note (US2YT=RR) yield, which typically moves in step with interest rate expectations for the Federal Reserve, was flat at 3.852 %, from 3.852 % late on Friday .

On Friday, while Wall Street markets were closed for the Good Friday holiday, the U.S. jobs report showed that employment growth rebounded more than expected in March, with a 178,000 increase in nonfarm payrolls representing the biggest increase in more than a year. The unemployment rate fell to 4.3% from 4.4%, as people dropped out of the workforce.

The data complicates the picture for the Federal Reserve, which will next decide on monetary policy at a two-day meeting ending on April 29. However, traders are not pricing in any rate cuts from the U.S. central bank until October 2027, according to the CME Group's Fedwatch tool.

Citing uncertainty around inflation and heightened geopolitical risks tied to the Middle East war, Wells Fargo Investment Institute said on Monday that it no longer expects a Fed rate cut in 2026 compared with its previous forecast for two cuts this year.

In precious metals , gold was little changed while silver dipped as market participants cautiously awaited further signals on the evolving U.S.-Iran situation and its impact on global interest rates.

Spot gold rose 0.1% to $ 4,680.29 an ounce and spot silver fell 0.37% to $ 72.71 an ounce.

data.title: Sumitomo Mitsui, Nippon Life Discuss 500 Billion Private Credit Fund
data.description:
Japan's private credit market could be approaching an inflection point, as Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group and Nippon Life Insurance are in discussions to establish a private credit fund with initial capital of at least 500 billion ($3.1 billion), according to people familiar with the matter. The two institutions are working through the structure of a potential joint venture that would manage the fund, which is expected to provide financing for leveraged buyouts, real estate transactions, and mezzanine deals. While key details such as final fund size and capital commitments remain under discussion, the structure could also allow additional investors to participate, potentially expanding the platform over time.

The backdrop is shifting. Demand for leveraged buyout financing appears to be rising as Japanese companies increasingly divest non-core businesses or consider going private following governance reforms aimed at improving shareholder accountability. Historically, lending for these transactions has been dominated by major banks including Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group and Mizuho Financial Group, but this initiative could open the door for insurers and other non-bank institutions to play a more meaningful role in deal financing. Regulators and industry participants have been exploring ways to broaden the lender base, which could help accommodate rising deal flow while potentially reducing concentration risks in the system.

At the same time, global alternative asset managers such as Apollo Global Management , Blackstone , and KKR & Co. have been building private credit capabilities in Tokyo to originate loans locally, although progress could be gradual. Against this backdrop, Sumitomo Mitsui's CEO Toru Nakashima has pointed to private credit as a potential growth area given the increase in buyouts, while Nippon Life continues to explore ways to diversify its more than 80 trillion investment portfolio beyond traditional bonds and equities. If executed, the fund could mark a notable step in the evolution of Japan's credit markets, potentially signaling a broader shift toward diversified sources of buyout financing.

data.title: White House says increased productivity means Fed can cut rates
data.description:
White House economic adviser Kevin Hassett told CNBC on Monday that he believes a "supply shock" in the U.S. economy caused by capital spending and higher productivity from artificial intelligence will allow the Federal Reserve to lower interest rates.

"If we have a supply shock like we're seeing because of all this capital spending ... AI increasing productivity, it puts downward, downward pressure on inflation, and that should take the pressure off the Fed. They should be able to lower rates," Hassett said.

Hassett said he expects interest rates will be lowered if Kevin Warsh, President Donald Trump's nominee to become Federal Reserve chair, takes the position.

data.title: Dubai Investments, JOOD sign MoU to advance community impact aligned with Dubai Social Agenda 33
data.description:
Staff Writer

Dubai Investments announced the signing of a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with Dubai Community Contributions Establishment - JOOD, an official Dubai‑based contribution entity established to enable and organise community contributions in line with the Dubai Social Agenda 33.

The MoU was signed between Dubai Investments and JOOD and the signing ceremony was attended by Khalid Bin Kalban, Vice Chairman and CEO of Dubai Investments; Mohammed Saeed Al Raqbani, Head of the Sustainability Committee at Dubai Investments and Marwan Rashed BinHashim, CEO of Dubai Community Contributions Establishment - JOOD. The MoU reflects a shared commitment to supporting verified social initiatives and strengthening community impact through structured, transparent and trusted contribution mechanisms.

Under the agreement, Dubai Investments will collaborate with JOOD to support community‑focused initiatives across key impact pillars, including healthcare, housing, education, sports, culture & arts and the environment.

The partnership aligns with Dubai Investments’ broader approach to responsible corporate engagement and meaningful social contribution.

Article Source: Emirates News Agency (WAM)

data.title: Why diversification may not protect you anymore when markets fall together
data.description:
Diversification has long been one of the most widely accepted principles in investing. The idea is simple: spread investments across asset classes so that weakness in one is offset by stability or gains in another. For years, this approach worked reasonably well because market cycles were clearer and relationships between asset classes were more predictable.

But the last few years have challenged this assumption. We have witnessed periods where equities and bonds, traditionally seen as natural diversifiers, have fallen together. In 2022, the S&P 500 fell roughly 18% while long-duration US Treasuries lost over 25% - one of the worst simultaneous drawdowns for both asset classes in decades. Closer to home, Indian gilt funds also saw mark-to-market losses during periods of sharp rate tightening, even as equity markets remained under pressure. While market outcomes varied across regions, these episodes highlighted how diversification benefits can weaken during periods of stress. Even during periods of rising markets, more recently in 2025 we saw several global market equities, bonds as well as commodities, all rising together with positive returns. This experience has prompted many of us to ask an uncomfortable but necessary question: is diversification still working?

The answer is not that diversification has failed, but that the environment in which it operates has changed. Inflation, which remained benign for much of the last two decades, has returned as a key driver of market behaviour. Interest rates have moved sharply and often unpredictably. At the same time, geopolitical developments and global liquidity conditions are influencing markets more frequently and more forcefully than before. These factors have reduced the reliability of historical correlations that portfolios were built around.

Data over longer periods shows that correlations between asset classes are not fixed - they tend to rise during periods of stress. During the Global Financial Crisis of 2008, for instance, equities, high-yield credit, real estate and commodities all sold off sharply together, leaving investors with far less protection than their diversified portfolios had seemed to promise. This means that when diversification is needed the most, it often appears to work the least. Simply holding multiple asset classes without understanding how they interact across market regimes can create a false sense of diversification. Many portfolios appear diversified on paper but remain exposed to the same underlying risks.

Over time, financial markets have also become increasingly interconnected. Liquidity conditions, central bank policy and global capital flows now influence multiple asset classes simultaneously. A telling example: when the US Federal Reserve began its aggressive rate hike cycle in 2022, the ripple effects were felt simultaneously across US equities, emerging market currencies, Indian bonds and gold - asset classes that investors had long treated as independent of one another. As a result, assets that historically behaved differently can move together during periods of stress.

This creates what might be called the illusion of diversification. Holding many assets does not necessarily reduce risk if those assets respond to the same macro drivers. Consider a portfolio spread across Indian IT stocks, US technology ETFs and global growth-oriented funds - it may look diversified by geography, but each holding is essentially a bet on the same underlying driver: global growth and risk appetite.

The focus therefore needs to shift from what assets we own to why we own them. Investors benefit from examining the underlying forces that drive returns across asset classes. For instance, equities, credit and certain real assets can all be sensitive to growth expectations and liquidity conditions. When those drivers weaken together, portfolios that appear diversified can experience similar drawdowns - as seen in 2020's initial COVID-driven sell-off, where Nifty 50, corporate bond spreads and even gold temporarily fell in unison before eventually diverging.

Valuations add another important dimension to this discussion. The diversification benefits of an asset class are not independent of the price at which it is owned. When valuations across markets become stretched simultaneously - as they did across global equities and credit heading into 2022 - correlations often rise during corrections as risk is repriced across markets.

In practice, diversification should go beyond simply increasing the number of holdings. Investors need to assess whether each allocation provides a distinct source of return and risk within the portfolio. An investor holding large-cap, mid-cap and small-cap equity funds, for example, may believe they are diversified - but in a broad market downturn driven by slowing growth, all three segments tend to fall together. When multiple exposures depend on the same macro driver, diversification may prove less effective than expected.

Ultimately, diversification should be viewed as an evolving process rather than a one-time allocation decision. Markets move through different cycles, correlations change, and portfolios must adapt accordingly.

In today's environment, successful diversification is less about owning more assets and more about combining them thoughtfully, so portfolios remain resilient across changing economic conditions.

(The author is Head of Passive Investments and Products at DSP Mutual Fund)

data.title: Tesla stock sinks 2% on Monday as analyst suggests caution
data.description:
Tesla shares attempted a rebound on Monday but failed to sustain momentum after last week’s disappointing delivery report.

The stock was down around 2% at $353.41 in early trading.

The broader market showed modest strength, with the Nasdaq up 0.3%, while the Dow Jones Industrial Average was flat.

Tesla shares had already declined 5.4% on Thursday ahead of the holiday weekend, following the release of first-quarter delivery figures that fell short of expectations.

Delivery miss and inventory build

Tesla reported deliveries of 358,023 vehicles in the first quarter, below Wall Street expectations of about 366,000 units.

The company also produced roughly 50,000 more vehicles than it delivered.

The resulting inventory build has raised concerns about future pricing pressure and potential production adjustments.

JPMorgan analyst Ryan Brinkman highlighted the issue in a note, stating that the “record surge in unsold vehicles adds to free cash flow woes.”

He noted that inventory ties up cash until it is sold.

Tesla has historically generated free cash flow, but that trend may not continue in the near term.

The company is expected to see negative free cash flow this year as capital expenditure rises sharply.

Spending is projected to reach about $20 billion, up from $8.5 billion last year.

Much of this investment is directed toward artificial intelligence initiatives, including robotics and other emerging technologies.

JPMorgan flags downside risks

JPMorgan reiterated its underweight rating on Tesla and maintained a $145 price target, implying significant downside from recent levels.

“We ... advise investors approach TSLA shares with a high degree of caution,” Brinkman said.

“Although both technology and execution risk seem substantially less than was once feared, expansion into higher volume segments with lower price points seems fraught with greater risk relative to demand, execution, and competition.”

The firm lowered its 2026 earnings per share estimate to $1.80 from $2, below consensus forecasts, after the delivery miss.

Brinkman added that while Tesla has a differentiated business model, strong product portfolio and leading technology, these positives are “more than offset by above-average execution risk, rising competition, growing controversy with regard to the brand, and valuation that seems to be pricing in a lot.”

Prolonged weakness in Tesla stock

Thursday’s decline marked Tesla’s seventh consecutive week of losses, with the stock falling about 14% over that period.

The downward trend began after the company reported better-than-expected fourth-quarter earnings in January.

Despite the strong results, the stock failed to gain traction, as investors looked for new catalysts.

Market participants are increasingly focused on Tesla’s AI-related opportunities, including autonomous vehicles and robotics, rather than near-term delivery performance.

Mixed analyst views persist

Not all analysts are equally cautious. Baird lowered its price target slightly to $538 from $548 while maintaining an Outperform rating.

The firm said weaker energy deployment figures could weigh on the stock in the near term.

It also noted that investor discussions have shifted away from core automotive metrics toward energy and broader strategic initiatives.

The divergence in views reflects the current positioning of Tesla.

While long-term expectations remain tied to AI and new business lines, near-term pressures from deliveries, inventory, and cash flow continue to weigh on sentiment.

data.title: U.S. Corn, Soybean Inspections Rise on Week
data.description:
By Anthony Harrup

Inspections of U.S. corn and soybeans for export rose last week and wheat was slightly lower, the Agriculture Department said Monday.

Corn inspections for the week ended April 2 were just above 2 million metric tons, up from 1.88 million tons the week before, and 1.61 million tons in the same week a year earlier, the USDA said. Marketing year-to-date corn inspections rose to 48.47 million tons from 35.68 million tons in the previous marketing year.

Soybean inspections last week were 779,352 tons, compared with 694,076 tons the week before, but below the 816,857 tons in the year-earlier week. Marketing year-to-date inspections were 30.67 million tons versus 41.59 million tons last year.

Wheat inspections declined to 334,106 tons from 386,254 tons the previous week, and were little changed from the year-earlier week's 335,388 tons. Marketing year-to-date wheat inspections reached 20.67 million tons compared with 17.73 million tons the previous year.

China was the top buyer of soybeans last week. Mexico and Japan were the leading destinations for corn, and Mexico was the main buyer of wheat.

To see related data, search "USDA Grain Inspections for Export in Metric Tons" in Dow Jones NewsPlus.

Write to Anthony Harrup at anthony.harrup@wsj.com
